title: You Are Being Lied To About Fish Oil

chunks.title: Concerns About Fish Oil Consumption
chunks.summary: The segment discusses the recommendation of high doses of fish oil and presents concerns over its safety, mentioning studies linking fish oil to increased rates of atrial fibrillation and arrythmia. It argues that fish oil may be overconsumed and compares its processing to that of seed oils, asserting both pose potential health risks.
chunks.transcript: Let's talk about fish oil for a moment and then I want to transition to some gut issues. I mean, this is gonna stir some people up. There's clear there's multiple studies with editorials I think in JAMAMA maybe in one of the journal of American Cardiology journals showing clearly increased rates of atrial fibrillation and arhythmia in humans taking four grams of fish oil per day plus and I just think yeah like you said it I hadn't thought about that way but that's pretty it's pretty pretty interesting like fish oil is the new seed oil and there's no reason for any of you guys listening to this podcast to be taking fish oil I just don't think you need it I think it's harm harming you for the same reasons. Fish oil increases LDL oxidation, but I wouldn't be surprised if there's actually data on that. Maybe not in humans, but certainly in in vitro, which would be suspect, and we need to make sure we look at that carefully. chunks.title: Differences Between Omega-3 and Omega-6 Fatty Acids
chunks.summary: This part explains the differences between omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids, particularly in terms of stability and their effects on health. The discussion highlights the inflammatory potential of omega-6s versus the perceived benefits of omega-3s, yet points out that omega-3s are highly susceptible to oxidation, raising concerns about their health effects.
chunks.transcript: >> Yeah, I I agree that it's over consumed. Uh I So, if we're going to compare omega-3s to omega sixes, there's a couple of key differences. One, omega-3s are typically less stable, more unsaturated with more double bonds, meaning that they're more susceptible to oxidation, not only when they're on the shelf, but also when they're in our tissues. So, that's one difference. The other is they produce different icosenoids which is the main reason why people have a different view of omega-3s from omega sixs where the omega sixs are considered to have inflammatory icosenoids whereas the omega-3s are considered not to be inflammatory or to be anti-inflammatory. We can come back to that. However, on the basis that the worst part of the omega sixes is the permeability and susceptible susceptibility to peroxidation both again on the shelf in our digestive systems in our blood and in our tissues. The omega-3s are that and worse because they are more susceptible to peroxidation. And so when looking at these uh studies involved in the membrane pacemaker theory of of living of aging, which is that the uh what I was mentioning earlier with the different levels of polyunsaturated fats and how that affects lifespan and aging and health, the omega-3s are worse typically because they have more double bonds and they're more they lead to more permeability and more susceptibility to oxidation than the omega sixes. And we if we just take fish oil, we see it very clearly there. Most fish oil is already oxidized on the shelf in levels beyond what's supposed to be regulatory standards. When we consume it, there's studies showing that just the digestive system alone will increase peroxidation substantially. And then we can look at studies looking at pharmaceutical grade fish oil in the triglyceride form. We can look at it in cod liver oil. We can look at it with antioxidants. And you still see increased levels of lipid peroxidation and some and especially worse disease processes when you see this play out over time in animals. Uh you see the lipid peroxidation in humans. And when you look at this in animals and you look at animals that are given higher levels of omega-3s, it shortens their lifespan and it increases lipid peroxidation and it interferes with mitochondrial respiration.

chunks.title: Impact of High Omega-3 Intake on Health
chunks.summary: The segment elaborates on the negative health implications of high omega-3 intake, citing studies that show it can increase lipid peroxidation, shorten lifespan in animals, and interfere with mitochondrial respiration. It emphasizes the need to be more vigilant about omega-3 consumption, including fatty fish choices.
